ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for White Bee Hoon or prawn rice vermicelli soup. FOODON_03315595 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite dishes - Pasta and noodle dishes
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodle dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Singaporean white bee hoon with prawns.

serving.natural_language_portion: One small serving of Big Prawns White Bee Hoon is approximately 450 g, comprising a bowl of rice vermicelli in milky prawn broth topped with 2–3 large whole prawns, suitable for one person.

nutrient_source.title: Rice vermicelli with seafood soup — composite dish estimation based on HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for bee hoon soup and prawn dishes
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore — Foodo / Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Big Prawns White Bee Hoon exists in HPB Foodo. Values are estimated by combining HPB entries for (1) bee hoon soup (rice vermicelli in broth) and (2) steamed/boiled prawns, weighted by approximate ingredient proportions in a 450 g serving. The milky broth characteristic of this dish (evaporated milk, lard) contributes additional fat and energy. Values represent a reasonable per-100 g estimate for the composite dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli (cooked) — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to white bee hoon dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cooked rice vermicelli has a reported GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database. White bee hoon is made from rice vermicelli; the milky broth, protein from prawns, and fat from lard are expected to moderate the glycemic response further. GI of 53 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 48.6) / 100 ≈ 25.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving ≈ 48.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1890 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load from the prawn broth and seasoning; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and no additional salt or soy sauce added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates (~25.7 GL per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the moderate glycemic load per serving; consuming a smaller portion, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables, and choosing the broth over the noodles can help manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and saturated fat from lard and evaporated milk in broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (~1890 mg per serving) and saturated fat from lard-based broth raises cardiovascular concern; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request reduced lard or broth.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Big Prawns White Bee Hoon (大虾白米粉) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood noodle dish featuring large whole prawns served in a rich, milky white broth over smooth rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The dish is a signature offering of establishments such as House of White Bee Hoon in Singapore. The broth is characteristically opaque and creamy, achieved by simmering prawn heads and shells with evaporated milk and rendered pork lard, producing a sweet, umami-rich, slightly fatty soup base. The rice vermicelli is silky and soft, absorbing the flavourful broth. Large fresh prawns are the centrepiece, typically served whole and shell-on, contributing a sweet, firm, and succulent texture. The dish is garnished with spring onions and white pepper. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein from prawns (~7.2 g per 100 g), moderate carbohydrate from rice vermicelli (~10.8 g per 100 g), and is relatively high in sodium (~420 mg per 100 g) due to the broth. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli GI data, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate (~25.7). The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork lard. It is a concern for individuals managing hypertension due to high sodium content per serving, and requires portion control for those with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Big Prawns White Bee Hoon exists in HPB Foodo or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining HPB Foodo reference values for bee hoon soup and boiled prawns, adjusted for the characteristic milky lard-based broth. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked rice vermicelli. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for bee hoon soup and prawn dishes; no exact match exists for this specific restaurant dish. 2. The dish contains pork lard and is therefore not halal. 3. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, and selenium are reported as -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo composite reference entries used for estimation. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo reference data for similar dishes — no industrial trans fats are expected in this traditionally prepared dish. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database; the actual GI of the composite dish may be lower due to fat and protein from broth and prawns moderating glycemic response. 6. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate for a small (S) portion based on typical hawker serving conventions; actual restaurant portion may vary. 7. The cuisine_tags in the input include 'BBQ' and 'Crab' which appear to be platform-level tags for the restaurant rather than descriptors of this specific dish; these have not been carried forward.
